title: Power Tools, Measurement Precision & Shop Culture

required.title: [Book]
required.author: Albert Jackson, David Day, and Simon Jennings

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: Stumpy Nubs — Power tool safety series

competence_markers.description: The student can articulate the specific dangers of each power tool introduced in this packet without reference to notes
The student demonstrates correct safety setup and PPE for each power tool before use — consistently, without prompting
The student operates each power tool with controlled technique and consistent safety compliance
The student measures and marks to within 1/16 inch accuracy using a tape measure, combination square, and caliper
The student maintains their tool kit: sharpens chisels, replaces blades, cleans and lubricates tools regularly
The student follows shop culture standards without reminders: cleanup, tool return, communication, respect for others' work
The student can write and use a pre-work safety checklist appropriate to the task
The student uses hearing protection, eye protection, and dust protection appropriately for each tool and operation

content.why_this_matters:
You have proven you can handle hand tools safely and with discipline. Now the stakes go up.

A hand saw cuts slowly enough that you can stop mid-stroke if something goes wrong. A circular saw completes its cut before your brain finishes processing the mistake. A hand drill gives you time to feel the bit grab. A drill press can snap a bit and send a fragment at your face in a fraction of a second. A chisel pushed by hand gives you control. An angle grinder spinning at 11,000 RPM gives you power — and removes your control margin almost entirely.

Power tools are not better tools. They are faster tools. They amplify everything you do — good technique and bad technique, careful attention and careless distraction. The person who uses power tools well is not the person who is unafraid of them. It is the person who understands exactly what each tool can do to them, and who maintains discipline under that knowledge.

This packet introduces power tools one at a time, each with its specific dangers understood before it is touched. It also raises your measurement standard from "close enough" to precision, introduces shop culture as a social discipline, and teaches you to maintain tools as a practice, not a chore.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Power tool competence** — safe, controlled operation of each major power tool with understanding of its specific risks
2. **Measurement precision** — consistent accuracy to 1/16 inch using multiple measuring instruments
3. **Safety reasoning** — understanding the physics behind each safety rule, not just the rule itself
4. **Shop culture** — the social discipline of shared workspace: mutual respect, shared responsibility, communication under noise
5. **Tool maintenance** — sharpening, blade replacement, lubrication, and storage as regular practice
6. **Workspace setup** — planning and organizing a workspace before beginning work, not improvising as you go
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Power Tool Principle

Every power tool operates on the same principle: a motor converts electrical energy into mechanical motion — rotation, oscillation, or reciprocation — at speeds and forces far beyond what your body can produce. This is what makes power tools useful. It is also what makes them dangerous.

A circular saw blade spins at approximately 5,000 RPM. At that speed, the blade's teeth are moving at roughly 120 miles per hour. A router bit spins at 20,000-25,000 RPM. An angle grinder disk spins at 10,000-11,000 RPM. At these speeds, the tool does not "cut" in the way a hand saw does — it removes material so fast that the process is essentially instantaneous. There is no time to react. There is no time to correct. The only protection is the discipline you apply before the tool starts.

This is why power tool safety is about preparation, not reaction. You cannot dodge a kickback. You cannot pull your hand away from a spinning blade faster than the blade can reach it. You can only ensure that your hands are never in the path, that the workpiece is secured, that the blade guard is in place, that you are standing in the right position. Safety is what you do before you pull the trigger.

### Part 2: Individual Power Tool Introduction

Each tool is introduced individually. You do not touch a tool until you can articulate its specific dangers and demonstrate the safety procedures for that tool.

**Cordless drill / drill press:**
- What it does: Drives screws and drills holes by rotating a bit at variable speed
- Specific dangers: Bit grabbing in the material and twisting the drill (wrist injury), hair or loose clothing catching in the chuck, bit breaking under lateral force (fragment ejection), drill press pulling the workpiece from your hands if not clamped
- Safety: Clamp workpieces on a drill press — never hand-hold. Tie back hair. No gloves near a drill press (the spinning chuck catches glove material and pulls your hand in). Select the correct bit and speed for the material. Let the tool do the work — do not force it.

**Circular saw:**
- What it does: A motor-driven circular blade cuts through wood along a straight line
- Specific dangers: Kickback — the blade binds in the cut and the saw jumps back toward the operator. This is the most common and most dangerous circular saw event. Also: blade contact (the blade extends below the material), cord cutting (cutting through your own power cord), and off-hand placement too close to the cut line.
- Safety: Support the workpiece so the cut-off piece falls away freely (binding causes kickback). Never reach under the material while cutting. Let the blade guard retract on its own — do not pin it back. Set blade depth to just below the material thickness. Stand to the side of the blade line, not directly behind it (if the saw kicks back, it travels straight back from the cut).

**Jigsaw:**
- What it does: A reciprocating blade cuts curves and irregular shapes
- Specific dangers: Blade breaking (brittle blades in hard material), cutting into surfaces below the workpiece, and the blade contacting your support hand
- Safety: Clamp the workpiece with the cut area overhanging the bench. Use the correct blade for the material. Keep your support hand well away from the underside of the cut.

**Random orbit sander:**
- What it does: Sands surfaces smooth through random orbital motion
- Specific dangers: Dust inhalation (wood dust is a genuine health hazard, especially from hardwoods), eye irritation from dust, work piece being pulled from your hands
- Safety: Dust mask is mandatory. Safety glasses are mandatory. Clamp the workpiece or hold it securely on a non-slip surface. Connect a dust collection system or vacuum when available.

**Angle grinder (introduction only — full use in 2.2.4):**
- What it does: Spins an abrasive or cutting disk at high speed for grinding, cutting, and polishing metal and other materials
- Specific dangers: Disk shattering (sending fragments at high velocity), kickback when the disk binds, grinding sparks igniting nearby materials, contact with the spinning disk
- Safety: Face shield, not just glasses. Leather gloves (for grinding, not for rotating tools that can catch). Check the disk rating against the grinder's RPM — never use a disk rated below the tool's speed. Never remove the guard. Clamp the workpiece.

**Table saw (observation and assisted use only at Weathering Storms):**
- What it does: A fixed blade protrudes through a flat table; you push the workpiece through the blade
- Specific dangers: Kickback (the most powerful and dangerous in any woodshop — a kickback from a table saw can launch a board across the room at lethal velocity), blade contact (the blade is always exposed above the table), and hand proximity to the blade during cuts
- Safety: Use the riving knife (prevents the kerf from closing on the blade). Use push sticks when the cut brings your hand within 6 inches of the blade. Never stand directly behind the workpiece. Never cut freehand — always use the fence or a miter gauge. Understand and respect the kickback zone.

### Part 3: Personal Protective Equipment — The Reasoning

PPE is not optional. It is also not arbitrary. Every piece of PPE exists because of a specific injury mechanism.

**Safety glasses:** Protect against flying fragments — wood chips, metal shavings, broken bits. The most common shop injury is an eye injury, and nearly all are preventable with glasses. Wear them any time any tool is in use — not just during your own operation, but any time anyone near you is creating debris.

**Hearing protection:** Power tools generate noise levels that cause permanent hearing damage with repeated exposure. A circular saw operates at approximately 100-110 decibels. Permanent hearing damage begins at sustained exposure above 85 decibels. Foam earplugs or earmuffs — every time.

**Dust mask or respirator:** Wood dust, especially from hardwoods like oak and walnut, is a carcinogen with prolonged exposure. Fine dust from sanding is invisible and stays airborne for hours. A dust mask is the minimum. A proper respirator is better for extended sanding or routing operations.

**Gloves — when and when not:** Leather gloves protect against abrasion, heat, and splinters during material handling, grinding, and welding. But gloves are dangerous near rotating machinery — a drill press, a lathe, a router. The spinning tool catches the glove and pulls your hand in faster than you can react. Rule: gloves for grinding and material handling. No gloves near anything that rotates.

### Part 4: Measurement Precision

At Stretching Branches, you learned to measure within 1/8 inch. That was the starting standard. Now the standard is 1/16 inch — and for some operations, closer.

**The tape measure in detail:** You already know the tape measure. Now learn to read it precisely. The smallest marks on a standard tape measure are 1/16 inch. Each inch is divided into 16 equal segments. Learn to read them without counting from the inch mark every time — 1/16, 1/8 (2/16), 3/16, 1/4 (4/16), 5/16, 3/8 (6/16), 7/16, 1/2 (8/16), and so on.

**The combination square:** More accurate than a tape measure for short measurements and for checking square and 45-degree angles. The ruler slides through the head and locks at any position. Use it to set consistent depths, mark lines parallel to an edge, and verify that corners are truly 90 degrees.

**The caliper:** Measures thickness, diameter, and depth to 1/1000 of an inch (with a dial caliper) or 1/128 inch (with a vernier caliper). Used when precision beyond a tape measure is required — fitting parts together, checking material thickness, verifying drill bit or dowel diameter.

**The protractor and angle finder:** Measures angles. When you need a cut at 37.5 degrees, not "about 40," these are the tools that make it possible.

**"Measure twice, cut once" is the minimum standard.** The real standard is: measure, mark, verify your mark, then cut. A measurement error costs you material, time, and sometimes an entire project. The five seconds it takes to verify a mark are always cheaper than the material you waste on a wrong cut.

### Part 5: Shop Culture

A shop is a shared space. Whether it is your family's garage or a community workshop, the culture of the shop determines whether it is safe, productive, and worth being in.

**Core principles:**
- Other people's projects are not touched without permission. Ever.
- Shared tools are returned to their correct location immediately after use — not at the end of the day, not when you are done with your project. Immediately.
- If you make a mess (sawdust, shavings, spills, cutoffs), you clean it up before you leave that station.
- If you break something — a tool, a bit, a blade — you report it immediately. You do not hide it, ignore it, or put it back and let the next person discover it.
- When someone is operating a power tool, you do not approach them from behind, startle them, or try to talk to them. You wait until they are done, the tool is off, and they have acknowledged you.
- Music is for low-danger tasks. When operating power tools, you need to hear the tool — changes in sound tell you when the cut is going well and when it is not. Headphones during power tool operation are not acceptable.

**Communication under noise:** Shops are loud. Develop clear hand signals with your mentor and any other people in the shop: stop, come here, look at this, I need help. A shop where people cannot communicate safely is a shop where accidents happen.

### Part 6: Tool Maintenance

A well-maintained tool performs better, lasts longer, and is safer than a neglected one. Maintenance is not a special event. It is what you do every time you use a tool.

**Blade sharpening:** Saw blades, chisel edges, and plane irons all require sharpening. A dull blade is dangerous (as you learned in Stretching Branches) and produces poor results. Learn to sharpen chisels on a sharpening stone — this is a fundamental skill. Saw blades are typically replaced or professionally sharpened.

**Blade and bit replacement:** Circular saw blades, jigsaw blades, drill bits, and sanding disks all wear out. Learn to recognize when a blade is dull (burning instead of cutting, requiring excessive force, producing rough surfaces) and how to replace it correctly. Always unplug the tool or remove the battery before changing a blade.

**Lubrication:** Moving parts need lubrication. Table saw adjustment mechanisms, drill press quills, and any sliding surfaces should be cleaned and lubricated according to the manufacturer's schedule.

**Rust prevention:** Metal tools rust. Wipe metal surfaces with a light coat of oil or paste wax after use. Store tools in a dry location. A rusted tool surface creates friction, stains workpieces, and shortens tool life.

**Cord and battery inspection:** Before each use, check power cords for damage — cuts, fraying, exposed wire. Check battery connections for corrosion. A damaged cord is an electrical hazard and a fire hazard.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Risk Articulation
**Developing well:** The student can describe what specifically makes each tool dangerous — not generic "it could cut you" but specific mechanisms: "The circular saw kicks back when the kerf closes on the blade because the spinning teeth grab the material and launch the saw backward." This level of understanding indicates that safety rules are internalized, not memorized.

**Developing slowly:** The student can list safety rules but cannot explain why they exist. "You have to use push sticks on the table saw" without understanding that the push stick keeps hands away from the blade and provides control if kickback occurs.

**Diagnostic questions:** When the student states a safety rule, ask "why?" If they can explain the physics, they understand. If they say "because that's the rule," they are memorizing, not understanding.

### 2. Safety Compliance
**Developing well:** PPE is on before the tool is touched. Workpiece is clamped before the tool is turned on. Guards are in place. The student checks all of these without being told. The critical test: do they maintain safety compliance when they think you are not watching? If possible, observe from a distance occasionally.

**Developing slowly:** The student forgets PPE, skips clamping "just this once," or rushes through setup to get to the cutting. Any of these is a stop-and-reset situation.

### 3. Measurement Precision
**Developing well:** Consistent accuracy to 1/16" on tape measure readings. Correct use of combination square for checking 90 degrees. Can read and use a caliper. Marks are clean, visible, and on the correct side of the measurement.

**Developing slowly:** Inconsistent readings, confusion about which mark on the tape represents which fraction, or marks that are thick and ambiguous. Measurement skill improves with practice, but persistent difficulty may indicate the student needs more work on fractions in their numeracy program.

### 4. Shop Culture
**Developing well:** Tools returned immediately after use. Cleanup is automatic. Respect for others' work and space is consistent. The student communicates appropriately in the shop environment — waits for power tools to stop before approaching, uses hand signals when necessary.

**Developing slowly:** Tools accumulate on the bench. Cleanup is prompted. The student interrupts others during power tool operation.
